proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Peregrino as an isometric survival management game centered on crossing a cursed land, scavenging, hunting, fighting, managing scarce resources, inventory pressure, nightfall danger, and extracting with loot. The horror identity is strong via grotesque abominations, cursed woods, and survival-focused threat escalation after dusk. The game also has clear party/caravan management elements: caring for companions, leading pilgrims, making fate-affecting decisions, and upgrading caravan crafting stations. That makes it broader than pure survival horror, so survival_horror fits best as the primary archetype with tactical_rpg and party_based_crpg as secondary influences rather than full identities.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on caravan leadership, harsh travel, supply management, crew welfare, route planning, and survival in a hostile world, though it is more narrative CRPG/trading oriented than horror-survival.

Excellent match on scarcity, shelter upkeep, survivor needs, day/night pressure, scavenging runs, and hard moral survival choices, though it lacks the anchor's caravan-trek structure and broader party expedition framing.
Strong caravan-journey and follower-management overlap with consequential survival decisions, but its identity is more narrative tactical RPG than systemic scavenging and survival management.

Good fit on leading a roaming party through a hostile world with camp upkeep, resources, crafting, and attrition, but the core loop is more open-world mercenary tactics than horror survival management.
Top-down horror survival with strong day-forage/night-shelter cadence, resource scarcity, crafting, and escalating darkness pressure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a short, single-player scrapbook puzzler built around sorting mementos, reassembling torn photos, and solving gentle logic/word puzzles across handcrafted pages while uncovering an authored story about friendship and aging. There is no combat, the tone is explicitly cozy and emotional, and interaction appears cursor-driven through scrapbook pages. While the copy does not strongly emphasize classic hidden-object searching, the closest supported taxonomy fit is hidden_object_puzzle because the core loop is light object-focused puzzle interaction in handcrafted scenes/pages with narrative progression.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: cozy single-player object manipulation, environmental organization, gentle puzzle structure, decoration, and intimate story revealed through personal belongings.
Excellent match on casual tactile sorting/arrangement puzzles and low-pressure play, though it is less narrative-led and less memoir-focused than the anchor.
Short, cozy, story-driven puzzle game centered on handling meaningful objects; the repair/disassembly focus differs, but the emotional authored progression is very compatible.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Behind the Frame: The Finest Scenery
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Behind the Frame: The Finest Scenery
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares brief relaxing pacing, light puzzles, hand-crafted progression, and emotional presentation, but plays more like interactive fiction/art puzzling than object-sorting scrapbook play.
Organization-first puzzle loop built on sorting, placing, and retrieving objects in a calm single-player format.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Mirage 7 as a third-person, single-player story adventure centered on Nadira’s journey through a desert setting, with authored narration, environmental puzzle solving, collectible-item crafting, and melee/ranged combat against monsters. That combination supports a cinematic action-adventure identity more strongly than RPG or open-world labels. World topology is not stated explicitly, but the copy emphasizes a guided tale and handcrafted challenges rather than sandbox or systemic play.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on third-person story-led action-adventure structure, environmental exploration, collectibles/upgrades, and mixed melee/ranged combat, with only somewhat faster and more combat-forward pacing.
Strong fit on third-person action-adventure, desert/sci-fi exploration, companion-enabled progression, and combat/puzzle gating, though it is more systems-heavy and overtly combat-centric.
Matches the mythic guided journey, young-protagonist focus, traversal, environmental puzzles, and emotional storytelling, but lacks Mirage 7's meaningful combat layer.

Another linear, narrative-heavy third-person adventure with crafted tools and environmental problem-solving, but its stealth-survival loop is a different gameplay identity from Mirage 7's exploration-plus-direct-combat mix.
Single-player cinematic adventure blending exploration, environmental challenges, crafting-lite survival elements, and ranged/melee encounters.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence consistently describes Fortuna Magus as a fantasy JRPG with a rich authored story, single-player campaign structure, and strategic turn-based party battles. Core gameplay details are concrete and repeated across Steam short/detailed descriptions and Metacritic: characters pursue a missing-father narrative, battles revolve around Revelations, TP management, tandem attacks, and learning new spells/skills through combat conditions and element thresholds. That supports a story-led JRPG identity with party management, magic-focused turn-based combat, quest-driven progression, and skill customization. Alternative candidates like turn_based_tactics are weaker because there is no evidence of grid tactics or overhead tactical structure, while monster_collect_rpg lacks creature-collection evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Story-focused turn-based JRPG with party synergy, special resource management, and lots of character build customization.
Linear narrative JRPG built around turn-based party combat, character skill progression, and tactical use of team abilities.
Classic-style JRPG with authored story progression, turn-based battles, and progression tied to skill and stat customization.
Very close Kemco-style match: traditional turn-based JRPG structure, party management, elemental growth systems, and skill customization.
Another straightforward story-driven Kemco JRPG with turn-based battles, party progression, and unlockable special abilities.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes a cute multiplayer pet-chaos game with co-op, PvP, and asymmetric roles (pets cause destruction, owner catches/defends), plus collectibles and a home-building mode. However, the material does not clearly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The strongest signals are party/co-op/social chaos, but the evidence does not concretely define a match-based party-game structure, nor does it support action-adventure, platformer, shooter, racer, sim, or RPG archetypes in the allowed list. The current taxonomy audit already flagged a provider gap with no candidate archetypes. Because the gameplay description is broad, mode-mixed, and not strongly aligned to a supported archetype, classification should be rejected for now.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit on co-op animal chaos, physics-driven destruction, casual controls, and mayhem in everyday spaces. Looser because it is broader open-world sandbox play rather than asymmetric household sabotage/defense.

Excellent thematic and mechanical overlap in pet-led indoor destruction and object interaction. Lower than Goat Simulator 3 because it is primarily single-player and lacks the anchor's multiplayer/asymmetric party identity.

Strong animal-mischief comparison with humans as playful obstacles and environments as prank spaces. Less direct because its core loop is stealthy task/prank puzzles, not destruction-heavy co-op/PvP chaos.

Matches accessible multiplayer chaos, cute animals, physics interactions, and party-session appeal. Weaker gameplay fit because it is fundamentally a brawler-party game focused on direct player combat rather than environmental wrecking and asymmetric sabotage.
Asymmetric chaser-vs-mischief format with props, environmental mayhem, capture pressure, and short multiplayer rounds.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Banquet for Fools as a single-player, open-world, party-based RPG centered on investigating a village disappearance across the island of Invimona. Core loop evidence is strong: exploring towns and holds for clues, taking quests/requests, managing four companions with stat-driven dialogue options and bonding, and progressing characters through skills, spells, songs, equipment, and level-ups. Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Large-scale party RPG with real-time combat plus tactical pause, companion relationships, open-area quest exploration, and strong party-management overlap despite being more cinematic and system-heavy.
Party-based narrative RPG with island exploration, companion interactions, and pause-driven tactical play; less of an action brawler and much more CRPG-shaped than the anchor.
Strong overlap in continuous real-time action RPG flow, party building, and progression, but it is far more combat-forward and less investigation-, dialogue-, and companion-choice-driven.
Companion-based action RPG focused on quests, diplomacy, and uncovering island mysteries through exploration and clue-led investigation.

current_live_neighbors.title: Atelier Ryza 3: Alchemist of the End & the Secret Key

removed_candidates: Final Fantasy XII: The Zodiac Age

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on run-based drafting, arcade-machine feel, kinetic board interactions, and absurd combo engines. Pachinko scoring differs from claw grabbing, but the physics-plus-synergy identity is highly compatible.

Excellent match on roguelike deckbuilder structure, randomized drafting, and payoff-combo construction inside a gambling-machine frame. Less tactile/physics-driven than Cupiclaw, but very aligned in buildcraft and run logic.

Shares physics-driven aiming, run progression, relic/orb synergies, and arcade roots. However, Peglin is fundamentally turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw's machine-management combo loop.
A run-based combo engine builder focused on drafting synergies and exploiting random systems for escalating score output.
Uses run-based drafting and spatial board optimization to create item combos, with progression driven by build shaping more than reflex combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor in the same series with nearly identical point-and-click horror-adventure structure, grotesque cartoon style, linear progression, and puzzle-led play.
Very close match on single-player horror point-and-click gameplay, cursor-driven puzzle solving, and story-led progression, though it leans more psychological and narrative-heavy.
Strong fit as a dark 2D point-and-click puzzle adventure with unsettling imagery, but its non-linear action-reaction design is less aligned with the anchor’s more authored linear flow.
Good interface and puzzle-investigation overlap, but the comedic episodic micro-case structure is a noticeable step away from the anchor’s grotesque linear horror adventure.
Dark point-and-click puzzle adventure with linear progression, creepy storybook horror, and puzzle-gated exploration.

title: Ratcheteer DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently describe an interconnected action-adventure with more than 250 rooms across multiple regions and dungeons, eight tools that unlock new paths, and explicit traversal verbs like gliding, dashing, drilling, and stomping. The structure of gated exploration through a connected world plus boss encounters strongly supports metroidvania. The copy also supports a 2D platform-navigation-forward presentation and single-player campaign progression.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D action-adventure with room-to-room exploration, dungeons, light combat, items unlocking deeper areas, and a nonlinear connected world; closest structural fit despite a more Zelda-like emphasis.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent benchmark for item-gated exploration, bespoke dungeons, and boss progression, but its top-down Zelda structure is less metroidvania/platform-forward than the anchor.
Interconnected world, secrets, bosses, and tool-based progression fit well, but its isometric combat-led action and heavier mastery/discovery layer make it a looser match.
2D exploration platformer centered on tool upgrades, drilling-based traversal, gated backtracking, and dungeon-style progression.
Side-view action-adventure with wrench-driven combat, ability-gated routes, bosses, and a strong dungeon-to-dungeon campaign flow.

missing_must_include_titles: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Docked as a realistic port-operations simulator centered on operating heavy machinery, completing mission-based jobs and contracts, repairing equipment, expanding infrastructure, purchasing lots and machinery, and growing a logistics business. That supports transport_sim most strongly, with a management layer as secondary rather than primary. The current computed taxonomy over-indexes on racing because of vehicle language, but the actual gameplay loop is commercial cargo handling and operational expansion, not racing competition.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: realistic machinery operation, mission-based contracts, campaign progression, fleet expansion, and building up a work-sim business from scratch.
Strong overlap in heavy equipment, restoration work, structured jobs, hauling materials, and rebuilding infrastructure through a company-management frame.
Good match on precision vehicle handling, cargo delivery, contracts, upgrades, and simulation challenge, though it is more off-road transport focused than site-operation and port-management focused.
Contract-driven cargo work with hands-on loading, heavy vehicles, precise maneuvering, and logistics-focused job flow.
Slow, technical heavy-vehicle operation built around difficult hauling jobs, vehicle control, and recovery-focused mission structure.

title: Poker Night at the Inventory

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a single-player No Limit Texas Hold'em poker game focused on card play, betting, unlockable table/deck cosmetics, and character banter. The current computed hidden_object_puzzle read appears to be a false positive driven by incidental 'point-and-click' wording in character/franchise references rather than the actual gameplay loop. Because the allowed taxonomy archetype list does not include a poker/gambling/card-table archetype, and the available archetypes would misrepresent the game's core identity, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares the core loop of repeated poker matches, readable opponent behavior, and presentation built around personality at the table.

unresolved_similar_games: World Series of Poker: Tournament of Champions

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present the game as a shop/store management sandbox first: designing the shop layout, organizing shelves, setting prices, managing staff, crafting stock and upgrades, and building barricades/traps. That strongly fits management_tycoon with systemic sandbox/base growth. The zombie defense layer is significant but appears additive to the management loop rather than the main identity; combat is described as defending the shop during raids and hordes, not as the core session structure. Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong overlap in recruiting/equipping survivors, base development, scavenging, and zombie-driven defense decisions. Weaker on the anchor's store-management, pricing, and retail optimization loop, so it is adjacent rather than truly same.
Hands-on shop layout, shelf placement, inventory flow, pricing, and customer-service management.
Distinctive shopkeeping paired with active combat and loot-driven restocking, echoing the management-plus-action hybrid.
Shopkeeping centered on valuation, pricing, customer interaction, and morally shaded merchant decisions.
Survivor community management, scavenging, crafting, and defending a growing base against zombie threats.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies the game as a roguelite tower defense: placing towers, walls, and traps, shaping enemy paths, surviving wave-based encounters, drafting Boon Cards, fighting boss enemies each run, and unlocking permanent upgrades between runs. That is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 does not include a tower defense / tower-defense roguelite archetype. Available archetypes such as rts, turn_based_tactics, card_battler, or co_op_action_roguelite would misrepresent the core play loop. Because the evidence is strong yet does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Roguelite tower defense focused on short runs, drafting build-defining pieces, and adapting your defense each playthrough.

title: Never Grave: The Witch and the Curse

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'Metroidvania x Roguelite' with side-scrolling 2D action, exploration emphasis, traversal unlocks like double-jump, dungeon obstacles and puzzles, and intense boss fights. The core loop also includes procedurally generated dungeon runs, loot/material collection, and village rebuilding with facilities, crops, cooking, and skill-tree growth, which supports roguelite metaprogression as a secondary identity rather than displacing the metroidvania core. Up to 4-player co-op is explicit, but the strongest gameplay identity remains exploration-driven action-platforming with ability-gated progression.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D metroidvania combat-platforming, procedural/world-shifting structure, traversal unlocks, bosses, and even up-to-4-player co-op; less run-based and less base-building than the anchor.

Excellent match for fast 2D combat-platforming, roguelite runs, procedural layouts, ability unlocks, and repeated progression; weaker on co-op and village-building, and more combat-run focused than exploration-led.
Strong side-scrolling roguelite comp with procedural runs and persistent home/manor growth, but it is more stage/run-driven and less metroidvania-exploration-focused, with no co-op.
Good fit for 2D exploration-platforming, ability gating, and procedurally assembled world structure, but it is more straightforward single-player metroidvania than roguelite co-op hybrid.
Procedural metroidvania runs with ability-gated exploration, side-scrolling combat, and a strong roguelite structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single promotional description emphasizing a cozy rebuilding premise: playing as Ditto, crafting, learning Pokémon moves, and restoring a desolate world. While this suggests buildcraft and systemic world-shaping, it does not concretely establish the core gameplay loop, perspective, session structure, management depth, or whether the title plays primarily as life sim, farming sim, city/colony builder, or another simulation form. With only sparse, high-level copy and no stronger gameplay-specific evidence, classification would be too speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on ruined-world restoration, hands-on gathering/crafting, terrain building, quest-driven progression, and settlement reconstruction in a character-scale sandbox.
Shares the loop of collecting resources, crafting infrastructure, completing town projects, and reviving a desolate settlement, though it is more workshop/life-sim and combat-forward.

Strong thematic and structural overlap in restoring a decayed world through quests, unlocking areas, crafting, decorating, and growing a community, but it is more life-sim/social management than systemic building sandbox.
Good fit for cozy progression, crafting, customization, terraforming, and community-building, but its day-to-day life-sim cadence is looser and less centered on directed world restoration/adventure.
Block-by-block building, crafting, exploration, and restoring ruined areas into functioning settlements.

title: Oceanhorn 3: Legend of the Shadow Sea

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only a broad open-world action/adventure premise: sailing across islands, battling giant Titans, and pursuing a long-lost god. However, the DB evidence is too sparse to confidently place it into a specific V2 archetype. Core identity axes needed for acceptance remain ungrounded, especially perspective, concrete combat style/tempo, progression model, and whether the RPG framing is substantial enough for open_world_fantasy_action_rpg versus broader open_world_action_adventure. With only a short Metacritic description and one normalized genre label, the classification would be too inferential.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Oceanhorn 2: Knights of the Lost Realm
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Oceanhorn 2: Knights of the Lost Realm
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same series foundation of Zelda-like action-adventure, puzzle-combat dungeons, exploration, and story-led progression.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Open-air exploration, gliding, freeform discovery, and quest-driven action-adventure progression.
Colorful open-world action-adventure with exploration-heavy questing, traversal abilities, and mythic enemy encounters.
Earlier series entry centered on island exploration, Zelda-inspired combat, environmental puzzles, and adventure progression.

unresolved_similar_games: The Legend of Zelda: The Wind Waker
missing_must_include_titles: Oceanhorn 2: Knights of the Lost Realm
The Legend of Zelda: The Wind Waker

title: Planet of Lana II: Children of the Leaf

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies the game as a 'cinematic puzzle adventure' with a sci-fi narrative focus and mentions increased puzzle complexity, but it does not provide enough concrete gameplay identity to map confidently to a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The available text does not firmly establish perspective, traversal model, platforming emphasis, stealth/combat structure, or whether it aligns more with action_platformer, cinematic_action_adventure, or another nearby archetype. Because the evidence is descriptive but too sparse on core playable structure, classification should be rejected.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The original game shares the same core cinematic puzzle-platforming structure, companion-based interactions, and sci-fi adventure identity.
A highly recognizable cinematic side-scrolling puzzle adventure focused on environmental problem-solving, tension, and wordless storytelling.
Another landmark cinematic puzzle-platformer built around traversal, timing, and environmental hazards in a tightly guided side-scrolling format.
Pairs sci-fi storytelling with cinematic side-scrolling puzzle progression and environmental navigation rather than combat-heavy action.
A close adjacent comp for cinematic 2.5D puzzle-platforming, guided progression, and companion-driven set pieces, though with a stronger horror emphasis.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Avenue Escape as a level-based traffic-management puzzle game: 50 stages, directing cars through intersections, timing moves, avoiding accidents, and optimizing clears. That supports a puzzle/logic identity, but the available taxonomy archetype list does not include a general traffic or route-planning puzzle category. The game does not strongly fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, pinball, rhythm_game, transport_sim, or other listed archetypes without forcing a mismatch.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Also centers on managing city traffic flow, timing signals, and guiding cars and pedestrians safely through busy intersections.
A road-traffic puzzle about optimizing vehicle movement through urban layouts and preventing jams through careful planning.
Shares the route-management, timing, and crash-avoidance puzzle loop, though applied to rail networks instead of cars.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions and labels consistently identify this as a realistic baseball simulation. Official copy repeatedly emphasizes 'real baseball,' lifelike stadium/player presentation, exhibition play, online matches, rankings, and team-building via TEAM EDIT with roster selection and tournament regulations. Source labels include 'Baseball Sim' and baseball theme, while Steam categories support single-player, PvP, co-op, and split-screen/social play. The gameplay loop is match-based competition with realistic sports simulation rather than party-game framing.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest mainstream comp: realistic licensed baseball, full game sim, exhibition play, online competition, and roster/team management.
Another direct modern baseball-sim neighbor with authentic presentation, head-to-head matches, and team-building progression.
Still centered on playing complete baseball games with local/online multiplayer, though it leans more arcade than sim.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy consistently describes an anime-style 3D action game centered on combo attacks, dodging/parrying, mixed close-range and ranged offense, and surviving bullet-hell projectile patterns against powerful demon-girl bosses. The emphasis on fast combo combat and boss-focused encounters strongly fits character_action. Campaign/story framing, single-player mode, weapon/equipment unlocks, and authored narrative further support this reading. No evidence points more strongly to soulslike, shooter, horror, or RPG-first structures.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Boss-rush action centered on parries, close-range offense, and heavy bullet-pattern dodging; structurally different, but extremely close on challenge feel and encounter design.
Very strong match on fast 3D melee/ranged combat and projectile-dense boss fights, though its open-world action-RPG structure is broader than the anchor's apparent focus.
Anime 3D combo action with evasive defense and flashy powers fits well, but it reads more like story-heavy action RPG spectacle than bullet-hell-driven boss action.
A solid pure character-action comparison for stylish combos, mobility, and aggressive offense, but it lacks the anchor's pronounced bullet-hell and boss-pattern identity.
Stylish 3D melee/ranged combo combat built around perfect dodges, heavy projectile pressure, and boss spectacle.
